slides.title: Introduction
slides.content:
Design is everywhere — in every screen you look at, every product you use, every environment you enter. Most of it you experience without noticing. The mark of good design is that it works so naturally you don't notice it's design at all.

Design and art overlap but have different goals. Art expresses; design communicates and functions. A painting succeeds if it creates an aesthetic or emotional experience. A design succeeds if it communicates information clearly, enables a user to accomplish a goal, or solves a problem effectively — while doing so with aesthetic coherence.

This course introduces the principles underlying all visual design, digital and otherwise.

slides.title: The Four Principles of Design
slides.content:
Robin Williams' influential book 'The Non-Designer's Design Book' identifies four foundational design principles — a practical entry point for anyone new to design thinking:

**Contrast:** Make different things look different. If two elements aren't the same, make them very different — not slightly different. High contrast creates visual interest; low contrast creates visual confusion.

**Repetition:** Repeat visual elements (colors, fonts, shapes, styles) throughout a piece to create visual unity and consistency. Repetition signals 'these things belong together.'

**Alignment:** Every element should have a visual connection to something else on the page. Arbitrary placement looks accidental; deliberate alignment looks intentional and organized.

**Proximity:** Group related items together. Physical closeness communicates conceptual relationship. Items far apart are perceived as unrelated.

slides.title: Did You Know?
slides.content:
Dieter Rams, the legendary German designer who shaped Braun's product aesthetic from the 1950s through the 1990s, articulated 'Ten Principles of Good Design' that remain foundational to product and interface design worldwide — and that directly influenced Apple's design philosophy under Jony Ive.

The principles include: 'Good design is innovative,' 'Good design makes a product useful,' 'Good design is aesthetic,' 'Good design is honest,' and most famously: 'Good design is as little design as possible.'

Steve Jobs kept a Braun electric shaver on his desk as a design reference. The visual DNA connecting a 1960s Braun radio to an early iPod is unmistakable. Rams' principles shaped the design of the most commercially successful consumer electronics in history — and are still the standard against which digital product design is measured.

slides.title: Typography Is Design
slides.content:
Typography — the selection, arrangement, and style of type — is one of the most powerful tools in any designer's toolkit, and the most often underestimated by non-designers.

A type choice communicates personality before a word is read. Serif fonts (with small feet at the ends of strokes — Times New Roman, Georgia) traditionally communicate authority, tradition, and trustworthiness. Sans-serif fonts (no serifs — Helvetica, Arial, Futura) communicate modernity, cleanliness, and accessibility. Display fonts communicate personality and should be used sparingly.

Typographic hierarchy — varying size, weight, and spacing to indicate importance — guides the reader's eye through information. A page with everything the same size has no hierarchy; a reader doesn't know where to start. A page with clear typographic hierarchy reads itself.

The best typographers follow this principle: choose a typeface for what it says, not just how it looks.

slides.title: White Space
slides.content:
**White space** (also called negative space in design) is the empty area in a design layout — the space between elements, around text, between images, in margins. Despite being 'empty,' white space is an active design element.

White space serves several functions:
- It creates visual breathing room, reducing cognitive load
- It signals quality and luxury — premium brands use more white space
- It improves readability — text with adequate white space is significantly easier to read
- It creates focus — isolation in white space increases visual importance

The principle: don't fill all available space. Amateur design tends to fear empty space and fill it. Professional design uses white space deliberately as a compositional tool.

Apple's marketing design, Dieter Rams' product design, and high-end print publication design all make generous, intentional use of white space as a signature of quality.

slides.title: Try This Today
slides.content:
Look at any designed object — a website homepage, a flyer, a product package, a magazine page — and analyze it with the four principles:

1. **Contrast:** Is it clear? Do different elements look sufficiently different? Or do things blend together confusingly?
2. **Repetition:** Do you see recurring visual elements that create unity? Or does it look like everything was designed separately?
3. **Alignment:** Are elements visually connected? Or do things appear placed randomly?
4. **Proximity:** Are related things near each other? Or are items that belong together scattered?

Now identify one design decision that is excellent — and one that could be improved. The ability to articulate why something isn't working is as important as noticing that it isn't.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'contrast' mean in Robin Williams' design principles, and why is 'slightly different' a design problem?
quizQuestions.optionA: Contrast means using opposing colors — avoiding monochromatic schemes that create visual boredom
quizQuestions.optionB: Contrast means making different things look very different — slightly different creates visual confusion rather than clear distinction, as the viewer's brain struggles to determine whether the similarity is intentional or an error
quizQuestions.optionC: Contrast refers to the ratio of text to white space — too little contrast between content and background reduces readability
quizQuestions.optionD: Contrast is about creating visual tension that captures and holds attention through opposition of weight, size, or color
quizQuestions.explanation: In design, if two elements aren't the same, make them very different — not slightly different. Slight differences look accidental and create visual confusion. The viewer's brain tries to determine whether similarity was intended, which creates cognitive noise. High contrast creates clear distinction and visual interest.

quizQuestions.question: What does Dieter Rams' principle 'Good design is as little design as possible' mean?
quizQuestions.optionA: Design should be created by small teams using minimal resources
quizQuestions.optionB: Good design removes everything unnecessary, leaving only what serves the user's need — the form emerges from function without decorative additions, producing clarity and elegance
quizQuestions.optionC: Minimal design styles (monochrome, simple geometry) are superior to complex visual approaches
quizQuestions.optionD: Design problems should be solved with the smallest number of design decisions to reduce the potential for inconsistency
quizQuestions.explanation: Rams' 'less but better' (weniger aber besser) means eliminating everything that doesn't serve purpose — form follows function without decorative addition. This produces clarity and elegance. It directly influenced Jony Ive's Apple design philosophy: the mark of great design is that it seems inevitable — nothing could be removed without loss.

quizQuestions.question: What is typographic hierarchy and why does it matter for readability?
quizQuestions.optionA: The historical order of typeface development — serif fonts preceding sans-serif, which preceded display fonts
quizQuestions.optionB: Varying size, weight, and spacing to signal relative importance — guiding the reader's eye through information. Without hierarchy, a reader doesn't know where to start; clear hierarchy makes a page readable without conscious effort
quizQuestions.optionC: The relationship between font size and line spacing that determines whether text is comfortable to read
quizQuestions.optionD: The organizational system for categorizing typefaces by their visual characteristics and appropriate uses
quizQuestions.explanation: Typographic hierarchy — variations in size, weight, spacing — signals what's most important and guides the eye through information. Without it, everything competes equally and the reader must work to find their entry point. Clear hierarchy makes pages self-directing — the eye naturally follows the visual structure.

slides.title: Introduction
slides.content:
Digital design tools have democratized creative practice. Software that once cost thousands of dollars (Photoshop, Illustrator) is now available through subscriptions, and free alternatives (GIMP, Inkscape, Canva, Figma's free tier) make professional-quality design accessible to anyone.

But tools are only as powerful as the understanding behind them. A professional knows which tool to choose for which task, understands the underlying concepts the tool implements, and can produce results across multiple tools because their knowledge is concept-based rather than software-specific.

This lesson introduces the conceptual foundations that make sense of digital design tools — and helps you choose the right tool for each task.

slides.title: Raster vs. Vector: The Fundamental Distinction
slides.content:
Every digital image is created with one of two fundamental approaches — and choosing the wrong one for your purpose creates problems that can't easily be fixed later:

**Raster images** (bitmaps) are made of pixels — a grid of color values. Photographs are raster. Raster images have finite resolution — they look sharp at original size but become blurry when enlarged. Best for: photographs, complex color images, digital painting, web graphics at specific sizes.

Tools: Photoshop, GIMP, Procreate, Affinity Photo.

**Vector images** use mathematical descriptions of shapes — lines, curves, and fills described by coordinates and formulas. They can be scaled to any size without quality loss. Best for: logos, icons, typography, anything that needs to be printed at multiple sizes.

Tools: Illustrator, Inkscape, Affinity Designer, Figma (for UI).

The rule: if it needs to scale — use vector. If it's a photograph — use raster.

slides.title: Did You Know?
slides.content:
Figma — the collaborative interface design tool that became the industry standard for UI/UX design — was founded in 2012 and by 2022 was acquired by Adobe for $20 billion (the acquisition was later blocked by regulators).

What made Figma remarkable: it was browser-based, making real-time collaboration possible in a field that had previously relied on desktop software and file sharing. Designers, developers, and stakeholders could all look at and comment on the same design file simultaneously — transforming how design teams worked.

Figma's success also reflected a broader shift: design was no longer a solo activity handed off to developers. 'Design in public' — transparent, collaborative, iterative — became the standard workflow. The tool embodied an entire philosophy about how design should work.

slides.title: The Design Workflow
slides.content:
Professional design follows a process that prevents the most common amateur mistakes:

**1. Define the problem:** What is this design supposed to accomplish? Who is it for? What does success look like?

**2. Research and gather:** Who are the users? What precedents exist? What content do you have to work with?

**3. Sketch and ideate:** Rapid, low-fidelity exploration of multiple directions — not in software, but on paper or whiteboard. Generate options before committing.

**4. Develop and refine:** Move the most promising directions into software. Iterate on feedback.

**5. Test:** Does it actually work for users? Design that looks good but confuses users has failed at its core task.

**6. Deliver:** Format correctly for the end use — different files for print, web, and mobile.

Skipping the early stages (especially problem definition and sketching) and going straight to software is the most common beginner mistake — it produces 'designed to look nice' rather than 'designed to work.'

slides.title: UI vs. UX Design
slides.content:
**UI design** (User Interface design) is the visual design of digital interfaces — the specific buttons, menus, icons, typography, color, and layout of a digital product. It answers: what does it look like?

**UX design** (User Experience design) is the broader design of how users interact with and experience a digital product — the user journey, information architecture, interaction patterns, and overall usability. It answers: how does it work and feel to use?

UI without UX: beautiful interfaces that are confusing to use. UX without UI: well-structured but ugly or generic interfaces.

Good digital product design requires both. Most design roles today expect familiarity with both, though specialists exist in each. The integration of the two — visual design that enhances usability, and usability that shapes visual choices — is what produces genuinely excellent digital products.

slides.title: Try This Today
slides.content:
Download or use any free design tool (Canva is the easiest entry point; Figma is free for individual use; GIMP is free for photo editing).

Create something simple using the four design principles:
1. A social media post for an imaginary event
2. A simple logo for a fictional brand
3. A one-page flyer or poster

Before you start in software:
- Write down what this design needs to DO (problem definition)
- Sketch 3 different layout options by hand (even rough thumbnails)
- Choose the strongest sketch, then implement it

Notice: the process (defining purpose, sketching multiple options, then executing) produces different results than opening software and designing by intuition. The constraint of sketching by hand first prevents 'default-software-look' output.

quizQuestions.question: When should you use vector format rather than raster for a design project?
quizQuestions.optionA: Whenever the design involves complex gradients or photographic realism
quizQuestions.optionB: When the design needs to scale to multiple sizes without quality loss — logos, icons, typography, print materials at various dimensions
quizQuestions.optionC: For all digital design, since vector files are smaller and more versatile than raster files
quizQuestions.optionD: When the design will be used exclusively on screen, since raster files are too large for print production
quizQuestions.explanation: Vector images use mathematical descriptions that scale infinitely without quality loss. Any design that needs to work at multiple sizes — logos, icons, print materials — should be vector. Photographs and complex digital paintings must be raster; logos and scalable graphics must be vector.

quizQuestions.question: What is the most common beginner design mistake, and what does it produce?
quizQuestions.optionA: Overusing decorative fonts that reduce legibility and communicate poor taste
quizQuestions.optionB: Going directly to software without defining the problem or sketching options first — producing design 'to look nice' rather than design that works, because the process skips the thinking that determines what 'working' means
quizQuestions.optionC: Using too many design elements that create visual noise rather than clear hierarchy
quizQuestions.optionD: Choosing inappropriate color schemes that don't match brand guidelines or user expectations
quizQuestions.explanation: Skipping problem definition and sketching to go directly to software produces 'designed to look nice' — attractive artifacts that may fail at their actual communicative or functional purpose. The early stages (define, research, sketch multiple directions) determine whether the design actually works.

quizQuestions.question: What is the distinction between UI and UX design?
quizQuestions.optionA: UI design is for mobile applications; UX design is for web applications
quizQuestions.optionB: UI design is the visual design of interfaces (what it looks like); UX design is how users experience and interact with a product (how it works) — good digital products require both working together
quizQuestions.optionC: UX design focuses on the technical architecture of digital products; UI design makes that architecture visually accessible
quizQuestions.optionD: UI is for consumer-facing design; UX is for internal enterprise tools where usability matters more than aesthetics
quizQuestions.explanation: UI = visual design (buttons, typography, color, layout). UX = user experience design (journey, information architecture, usability, interaction patterns). UI without UX: beautiful but confusing. UX without UI: functional but generic. Great digital products require both working together.

slides.title: Introduction
slides.content:
Digital art is not simply traditional art made on a computer. It's a medium with its own properties, possibilities, and constraints — one that has fundamentally changed what artists can make, how quickly they can iterate, and who can access creative practice.

A digital canvas doesn't run out of paint. You can undo anything. You can duplicate, transform, and experiment with zero material cost. You can work at scales and speeds impossible in traditional media. You can combine photography, drawing, painting, and typography in a single work.

These freedoms come with a challenge: the unlimited undo button can become an obstacle to committing, to accepting the unpredictable marks that make traditional media work exciting. Digital art practice involves navigating these advantages and limitations deliberately.

slides.title: Digital Painting and Illustration
slides.content:
Digital painting software (Procreate on iPad, Clip Studio Paint, Photoshop) simulates the behavior of traditional media — brushes that respond to pressure and angle, texture that mimics canvas or paper, color mixing that approximates pigment mixing.

The fundamental workflow mirrors traditional painting:
- Sketch layer (light, rough, to establish composition)
- Block-in layer (establish major shapes and values)
- Develop layer (refine forms, add detail)
- Rendering layer (final detail, texture, lighting effects)

Digital advantages: infinite undo, layers that allow working on different elements independently, instant color adjustment, scale flexibility, no drying time.

The skill that transfers directly from traditional art: seeing, understanding light and form, compositional thinking, color sense. These must be developed — software doesn't provide them.

slides.title: Did You Know?
slides.content:
The CGI effects in James Cameron's Avatar (2009) required a crew of 1,000 artists working for four years to produce approximately 2,700 visual effects shots. The film created an entirely computer-generated world — environments, creatures, and characters — at a level of photorealism previously impossible.

But the more surprising fact about digital art in film: many 'computer-generated' characters still begin with physical maquettes (scale models), traditional character design sketches, and reference photography of real actors and environments. Digital artists study traditional figure drawing and life drawing for the same reason digital painters do: the computer can render anything you can describe — but you have to know what you're describing.

The core artistic skills predate the tools by millennia.

slides.title: AI-Generated Art: New Tool or New Category?
slides.content:
Since 2022, AI image generation (Midjourney, DALL-E, Stable Diffusion) has produced work of striking visual quality from text prompts — challenging assumptions about skill, authorship, and the nature of art-making.

The debate is genuinely complex:

**Arguments that AI art is art:** Human creative intent guides the prompts. Choosing among outputs, combining, editing, and contextualizing is curation — itself a creative act. Art's history includes many tools that extended creative reach beyond manual skill.

**Arguments that it's different:** AI systems were trained on artists' work without permission or compensation. The relationship between skill, effort, and output is different — a text prompt produces what a traditional artist's years of practice would. The 'artistic voice' discussion becomes complicated when outputs are statistical composites of many voices.

What's clear: AI tools are changing who can create, what can be created, and how quickly — with implications for art education, creative industries, and copyright law that are still being worked out.

slides.title: Generative Art
slides.content:
**Generative art** is art that is created using an autonomous system — a set of rules, algorithms, or processes that generate works independently or semi-independently from the artist's direct mark-making.

Generative art predates AI: John Cage's chance compositions using the I Ching (1950s), Sol LeWitt's wall drawings (executed from written instructions by others), Vera Molnár's computer-generated drawings (1960s), and fractal art (1980s-90s) are all generative.

The artist's creative act in generative work is: designing the system, setting the parameters, choosing which outputs to accept or discard. The system does the execution. This shifts questions of artistic creation from manual skill to conceptual and systemic design — which some find liberating and others find problematic.

Generative art has produced some of the most innovative and mathematically sophisticated visual art of the past 50 years.

slides.title: Try This Today
slides.content:
Try creating in a digital medium today. Several free options:

**iPad/Phone:** Procreate Pocket (paid but inexpensive) or Adobe Fresco (free tier) — draw or paint directly with your finger

**Browser-based:** Google's AutoDraw (AI assists your sketches) or Sketchpad (free digital drawing)

**AI-assisted:** Midjourney's free trial or DALL-E 3 in Bing Image Creator (free) — generate images from text prompts

For any of these:
1. Try to make something you had a clear mental image of — notice how well the tool serves your vision
2. Try to let the tool surprise you — accept an unexpected result and build on it
3. Notice: does the digital medium change what you make, compared to what you'd make with pencil and paper?

The medium shapes the message. Digital tools have their own affordances and resistances — discover yours.

quizQuestions.question: What skills from traditional art education transfer directly to digital art practice?
quizQuestions.optionA: Color mixing techniques — digital mixing simulates traditional pigment behavior closely enough that the skills transfer
quizQuestions.optionB: Seeing, understanding light and form, compositional thinking, and color sense — the foundational perceptual skills that software cannot provide and that predate all tools
quizQuestions.optionC: Brush handling and pressure control — modern tablets with styluses closely simulate traditional brush behavior
quizQuestions.optionD: Material knowledge — understanding how different digital brushes simulate different physical media
quizQuestions.explanation: Seeing, light and form understanding, composition, and color sense must be developed independently — software doesn't provide them. This is why digital artists study traditional figure drawing and life drawing: the computer renders anything you can describe, but you have to know what you're describing. Core artistic skills predate all tools.

quizQuestions.question: What is generative art, and how does it shift the definition of artistic creation?
quizQuestions.optionA: Art that generates an emotional response — defined by its effect on viewers rather than its production method
quizQuestions.optionB: Art created using autonomous systems (algorithms, rules, AI) that execute independently — shifting the artist's creative act from direct mark-making to designing the system, setting parameters, and selecting outputs
quizQuestions.optionC: Art that generates commercial value — successful art that enters the market through gallery or auction sales
quizQuestions.optionD: Art that generates other art — work that explicitly reproduces or transforms existing artworks as its primary creative strategy
quizQuestions.explanation: Generative art uses autonomous systems — from I Ching-based chance methods to algorithms to AI — where the artist designs the system rather than executing each mark. This shifts creative agency from manual skill to conceptual/systemic design, predating AI by decades and raising philosophical questions about authorship and intent.

quizQuestions.question: What are the strongest arguments on both sides of the AI-generated art debate?
quizQuestions.optionA: Pro: AI is a tool like any other. Con: AI tools are too expensive for most artists to access, creating equity problems
quizQuestions.optionB: Pro: human creative intent guides prompts and curation of outputs; art has always extended beyond manual skill. Con: AI was trained on artists' work without compensation; the relationship between skill/effort and output is fundamentally different; 'voice' is complicated by statistical averaging
quizQuestions.optionC: Pro: AI makes art accessible to non-artists. Con: this devalues artistic training and skill development
quizQuestions.optionD: Pro: AI art expands creative possibility beyond human imagination. Con: AI art lacks the intentionality and meaning that comes from human lived experience
quizQuestions.explanation: The strongest arguments: Pro — human intent guides prompts; curation is a creative act; tools have always extended creative reach. Con — training data use without compensation; the skill/effort/output relationship is different; outputs are statistical composites of many artists' voices. Both are substantive, which is why the debate continues.

lessons.title: Design Thinking and the World Around You

slides.title: Introduction
slides.content:
Design is not confined to screens and studios. It shapes every physical experience: the doorknob you turn, the chair you sit in, the signage that guides you through a hospital, the medication packaging that prevents dangerous errors, the urban design that makes a neighborhood safe or dangerous for pedestrians.

Design thinking — a human-centered approach to problem-solving that draws on design methods — has been applied to healthcare, education, public policy, and social innovation. Understanding what design is and how it works gives you a framework for seeing and improving the designed world you inhabit.

slides.title: Human-Centered Design
slides.content:
Human-centered design (HCD), developed and popularized by IDEO and Stanford's d.school, is a creative problem-solving approach that begins with deep understanding of the people for whom you're designing.

The five stages:
1. **Empathize:** Research the people you're designing for. Observe, interview, and understand their actual needs and experiences — not your assumptions about them.
2. **Define:** Articulate the problem clearly. The 'point of view statement': [User] needs [need] because [insight].
3. **Ideate:** Generate a wide range of creative solutions without judgment.
4. **Prototype:** Build rough, fast, cheap versions of your best ideas.
5. **Test:** Put prototypes in front of real users. Observe what works, what doesn't, what you didn't anticipate.

The process is explicitly iterative — you'll cycle through these stages multiple times as you learn from testing.

slides.title: Did You Know?
slides.content:
Donald Norman's 'The Design of Everyday Things' (1988, revised 2013) — one of the most influential books on design — begins with a simple question: why do people push doors that say 'pull'?

Norman's answer: it's not user error — it's design error. If a door requires a sign telling you which way to open it, the design has failed. Good design communicates its affordances (what actions are possible and how) without instructions.

Norman coined 'The Norman Door' — a door that's difficult to use because its design gives you the wrong signal about how it works. The term has since been applied to any designed object that is harder to use than it should be.

The lesson: when a user makes a 'mistake,' design thinking asks first whether it's actually a design failure — not a user failure.

slides.title: Design Failures and What They Teach
slides.content:
Bad design has real consequences. Three famous examples:

**The QWERTY keyboard:** Originally designed to slow typists down (to prevent typewriter jams by separating commonly paired letters). The mechanical problem it solved disappeared decades ago — but QWERTY persists through path dependence. Most typing speed limits are designed in.

**Hospital medication errors:** Research shows that many hospital medication errors result not from negligent staff but from packaging that makes similar medications look identical. Redesigning drug packaging has reduced errors significantly — design intervention, not training intervention.

**Voting ballots:** The 'butterfly ballot' in Florida during the 2000 US presidential election is a famous example: its confusing layout likely caused thousands of voters to punch the wrong candidate. An election's outcome was potentially altered by bad design.

Design is not decoration. It affects behavior, safety, outcomes — sometimes profoundly.

slides.title: Affordances
slides.content:
An **affordance** is a property of an object or environment that signals how it should be used — what actions it invites or enables.

The term was coined by psychologist James Gibson to describe the relationship between an animal and its environment. Designer Donald Norman adapted it to describe how designed objects communicate their possible uses: a round doorknob affords turning; a flat plate affords pushing; a handle affords pulling.

When affordances are clear, users interact with objects intuitively without instructions. When affordances are misleading or absent, users make 'errors' — but Norman's argument is that this is a design failure, not a user failure. The object gave the wrong signal.

In digital interfaces, affordances signal which elements are interactive (buttons appear button-like; links appear underlined or colored), how to interact (a drag handle invites dragging), and what will happen (a red button signals danger or stopping).

slides.title: Try This Today
slides.content:
Design audit of your physical environment:

Walk through one room or one daily route and identify:
1. One design that works invisibly — so naturally you never notice it
2. One 'Norman Door' — something designed in a way that makes you work harder than necessary or makes errors likely
3. One affordance — an object that clearly communicates how it should be used through its form

Then pick the worst design failure you found: How would you redesign it? What's the user's actual need? What's the simplest design that meets that need clearly?

Design thinking is a way of seeing the world — noticing what works, what doesn't, and why. Once you start seeing it, you can't stop.

quizQuestions.question: What is a 'Norman Door' and what design principle does it illustrate?
quizQuestions.optionA: A door design by architect Norman Foster that prioritizes aesthetic appeal over functional clarity
quizQuestions.optionB: A door that is difficult to use because its design gives incorrect signals about how to open it — illustrating that when users make 'errors,' design thinking asks whether it's actually a design failure rather than user failure
quizQuestions.optionC: A door with a concealed mechanism — used as a metaphor for products that hide their complexity behind a simple surface
quizQuestions.optionD: A legacy design that persists because of path dependence despite becoming functionally obsolete
quizQuestions.explanation: Norman's insight: if you need a sign saying 'push' or 'pull,' the design has failed — good design communicates its affordances without instructions. A Norman Door gave the wrong signal (a bar you'd naturally push, but must pull). When users are confused by design, the default question should be whether the design is at fault.

quizQuestions.question: What are affordances and why do they matter for design?
quizQuestions.optionA: The cost constraints that determine what design solutions are financially accessible to implement
quizQuestions.optionB: Properties of objects or environments that signal how they should be used — when clear, users interact intuitively without instructions; when misleading or absent, apparent 'user errors' are actually design failures
quizQuestions.optionC: The range of users that a design must accommodate — its accessibility spectrum from expert to novice
quizQuestions.optionD: The functional capabilities of a design system — what it can and cannot do
quizQuestions.explanation: Affordances (Gibson, adapted by Norman) signal possible actions: a handle affords pulling; a flat plate affords pushing; a clickable button appears button-like. Clear affordances make design intuitive. Misleading affordances create 'user errors' that are actually design failures — the object gave the wrong signal about how it works.

quizQuestions.question: What does the hospital medication packaging example demonstrate about design's real-world consequences?
quizQuestions.optionA: That pharmaceutical companies prioritize brand identity over user safety in packaging design
quizQuestions.optionB: That many apparent human errors in high-stakes environments are actually design failures — redesigning drug packaging (not retraining staff) has measurably reduced medication errors
quizQuestions.optionC: That regulatory bodies need to impose stricter design standards in safety-critical industries
quizQuestions.optionD: That training and design work together — neither alone is sufficient for safety in complex environments
quizQuestions.explanation: Medication errors from similar-looking packaging aren't solved by training — they're solved by design. When similar drugs look visually distinct, errors decrease. This illustrates that many apparent human errors in safety-critical environments are actually design failures that design interventions (not human interventions) can fix.
